item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The description takes far too long to get to the anomalous effects — paragraphs are wasted detailing specific dimensions and features that a reader either doesn't care about or can tell from the picture. Following this, there's far, far too many tests listed. My eyes began to glaze over at number seven or eight, and that's not even halfway.

Beyond that, the dialogue is extremely stilted and characters lack anything to make them unique. The final message doesn't bring anything new to the table either; the ethics of the Foundation are a topic that's reasonably well-trodden ground and just attaching that on without any new angles or takes on the issue makes your ending feel meaningless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Something that I think would be important to remember in future articles is that your characters should not ALL speak in the same "voice." That's one of the harder things to learn in writing, but if you think about how say, your parents speak differently than your friends, and your friends and coworkers speak differently than the person who is your boss's boss, it might help.

So while you may have an excuse to make the researchers sound similar (corporate culture influences behavior, and everybody tries to fit in at work, it makes a bit of sense for them to sound similar since they are of similar educational backgrounds and all have the same job) the D-class really need to have a very different tone in almost every case. Most death row inmates didn't go to university, statistically. They didn't have the same upbringing, they aren't in the same social class, and they don't have the same respect for authority or scientific language. Try to make future D-Class in future writings sound like someone that fits the profile, maybe from your life or maybe from TV. Not a direct copy, but think "what would ___say about this situation?" and use a reference of a low-level criminal on TV or someone who speaks very casually in your life (a teenager maybe! Or a rebel of some kind.) The spelling would all still be correct, because it's written up by low level researchers, but the words would be less formal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is really good! I have a couple qualms to make this go from a reluctant upvote to a definite upvote:

Typos/Grammar -
outpjtted - outputted
Dr Hope - Dr. Hope
level-3 - Level 3, as per http://www.scp-wiki.net/security-clearance-levels
the screen - the screen.
Log 4865-D has inconsistent formatting

There's a lack of clear and concise tone in log N, and it feels odd to read.
Lack of clinical tone also occurs in log R: "there was a small note on the camera though" remove the "though"/rewrite the sentence.

The Foundation referring to the people in the safe by their names feels weird and awkward. So does the dialogue between them.

The very last sentence of the article, about 4865-1 is weird and redundant. What is it? It teleported the penny, and it's now SCP-4865-1? That could at least be mentioned in the containment procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Multiple punctuation and spelling errors and improper formatting in the interview logs. The test logs go on for far longer than is necessary. The corrosion effect seems unnecessary. The writing in general is awkward and contrived. Finally, I feel like this kind of object has been done before.

Not totally sure why everyone is so amused at the SCP-SAFE-J reference. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well I like this brilliant idea!A safe safe classified as SAFE……
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
vaguely similar to SCP-1162 but still a nice SCP concept and a great article.
+1

item.title: Cheese trimmin
item.content:encoded: Otherwise great SCP but the D-1095 dialogue and drone exploration segment are a bit cheesy. Especially the latter, it seems strange that the researchers would use names instead of POI-4856. +1 regardless

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When I started reading it, it was fine. Expirement C gave some background to it, or story if you may, but other than that this is kind of weird. The eyes? Not sure what you were going for there but alright. There are also some small details that make this a bit worn down, like the fact that Timothy raped 80 women and children, kind of not believable, even if its supposed to be extreme. Or the fact the the D class were, how do I say it, “Too nice?” They spoke with some sense of professionalism, which makes them seem to not be convicts. Other than all that, I can see this being a nice SCP. For now, no vote. why weren’t the people that witnessed the item recovery amnestied?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is OK. I understand completely if it makes the Foundation sound silly. I am the kind of person who likes criticism and so I would like to improve should I ever make another. Again, thank you for the criticism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Your article have a problem in the formating in the test logs on mobile.

There is absolute no sense in use D-class subjects to perform the experiments when personnel could perform them without problems, especially when the D-classes are the problem.
Seriously? The foundation allow them to keep the items at will?
You have extremely non disciplined civilians drawing itens of a safe without proper control, one of them even drewn a weapon and killed himself. The foundation do commit errors, but in any manner I would think of them being so irresponsible.

Comments about the test log:

+ SHOW
- HIDE

Drugs.
Subject impeded from using them.

Ok, fair enough.

A killogram of various confectionery items.
and were promptly divided between the various researchers and other site personnel.

So no drugs, but candy is ok?!
Plus, what if it blewns someone's heads up? For god sake it was drew from a anomaly.

A apple coated in popping candy.
The item was confirmed to be safe and returned to D-2003.

Again, no drugs but candy allowed.

Upon consuming a small section of the apple, D-2003's mouth exploded and tore the skin at the cheeks.

Don't say I don't warned…
Wait, no one gives a s*?

The log is well humoured but that's the only positive point.
It's not much more than a bunch of people messing around with a anomaly, even after severe unacceptable accidents it seems no consequence are met and there is no change in the future tests.

Please, don't forget to be professional when referring to actions within the foundation. Consider some points:

+ show block
– hide block
  • The Foundation is a serious organization with decades, if not centuries, of experience in procedures of containment and testing of anomalous objects, entities and phenomena.
  • If a action have bad consequences the Foundation will likely try to prevent them in the future.
  • If you don't know something do not distribute it to your coworkers, especially when it can blown they heads up.
  • D-classes are disposable personnel, in direct words, we don't care about them. They are likely just being given amnestics after the experiments and terminated when they are not useful anymore. If a test are likely to kill or leave any consequences to the subjects you will use D-classes.
  • If a task require obedience and are to be controlled you'd rather use personnel instead.
  • You are allowed to mess around, but you are supposed to clean it afterwards.

As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

That's it, thank for attention. I recommend you don't take it personally. I do like humoured scps, but that article seems to make the Foundation very silly.

Edit: Thank you Zyn, it will be remembered.

item.title: Re: All good except...
item.content:encoded:
Hello!

I have read your comment, and would like to address your concerns to better the article. Could you please clarify where these are, as I have cleared out the ones that I can find, and weeding out the remaining errors would be beneficial.

item.title: All good except...
item.content:encoded: Very interesting concept and it has some interesting elements to it but there are a ton of spelling and grammar errors near the end, especially in the dialogue. No vote for now.

…
…
It's a pretty, SAFE SAFE XD
Sorry, I love this SCP

item.title: Re: yeah +1
item.content:encoded:
Safe dose not mean the item itself is harmless it just means its easy to contain.
Euclid is usually given to object that can move on its own or use some type of locomotion.
as 4865 can not move it is safe.

item.title: Re: SCP-4865
item.content:encoded: SCP-SAFE-j

item.title: Small criticism
item.content:encoded:
This is a really neat concept and I really enjoyed reading it! My only issue is with some of the dialogue.

D-1095: The pupil dilated in the shape of a smile. It looks quite menacing. You know the creepy smiles that the villains give in horror movies? Just like that!

This doesn't really come across as something somebody would actually say, let alone a D-class.
A lot of the dialogue in the article is this same way, it's just kind of awkward to read.

Other than that, great job!

item.title: This is pretty rad
item.content:encoded: This is super good for your first SCP. My only real nitpick is that the dialogue could use more contractions. Most of the time, no one actually says "it is" instead of "it's" anymore.

item.title: yeah +1
item.content:encoded: this scp is sick but i would give it euclid class

item.title: A Test log would be very nice
item.content:encoded: It would be very interesting if you made a test log for users to contribute too for this SCP

item.title: Re: SCP-4865
item.content:encoded: Ha ha.

item.title: Re: SCP-4865
item.content:encoded: I liked it but honestly I laughed at object class: safe

item.title: What the bread said.
item.content:encoded: I'm seconding what Deadly Bread said about the article's tone and syntax. The clinical tone is lacking in several areas of the piece, and Deadly Bread highlighted just about all of what I had in mind. I do like this skip, but it does need some revision.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for the criticism. It is something I can use to do something better. I may do some tweaking based on what you have written. I am surprised nobody picked upon this before I actually posted it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First of all, I like this concept. This is nice, especially for a first time scip. I’m just prefacing this with that so you know I don’t dislike the article. There are just some phrasing issues that I’d like to point out so they can be improved. Also, I’m not going to be covering the interviews because I’m admittedly not the best with actual dialogue so I wouldn’t be able to provide the best suggestions.

+ Crit
- Here’s the crit yes

Testing may be performed by Class-D Personnel or Level Three Site Personnel strictly.

The use of both “may” and “strictly” in the same procedure makes it sound a bit off. I’d suggest just cutting out both from the sentence.

Following the use of SCP-4865, the person who used it must record what they obtained from the object before exiting the room

I would just simplify this down to “All objects produced by SCP-4865 are to be documented, along with the subject who retrieved the item.”

The key lock on the door is broken, and no key will fit in the lock. This does not matter, as SCP-4865 is currently in an unlocked state.

This sentence feels a bit unnecessary and could probably just be incorporated into the description by saying it has a broken lock.

The bottom of SCP-4865 is extremely corroded, yet has not lost any of its structural strength. The corrosion also has an effect on what SCP-4865 is placed upon, however it usually does not suffer effects from the corrosion.

This is more of a personal nitpick, but the corrosion effect feels a bit tacked on. The SCP seems like it wants to be a gift giving safe with another works and religion on the other side, but “Hey also the bottom corrodes things.”

If the door is opened without the requirements to trigger an anomalous result

The wording is a bit weird here. I’d suggest just changing it to “When SCP-4865 is opened normally,”

It is also unknown how SCP-4865 selects what to give the person who opens it. The reason for this is currently the subject of ongoing research.

I think it would look better if this was merged with the section above.

There are nineteen objects obtained from SCP-4865 that have been publicly released for examination by researchers, post containment. These are labelled Product-A to Product-S.

I don’t think you need to say the amount of items here because they could just be counted in the log. I’d rephrase the sentence as just, “A log of objects obtained from SCP-4865, which are to be referred to as Product-A through Product-S, has been included below.” Also, big of a personal preference here, but I’d suggest changing them from things like Product-A to SCP-4865-A.

26th August

I’d put the month before the date.

One revolver, with bullets already loaded in the weapon.

Could probably change this to “One loaded revolver.”

This confirmed SCP-4865 uses the currency of the UK despite not being located in the UK at the time.

This should be reworded into something similar to, “This confirmed that currency SCP-4865 will be in pounds, despite not currently being located within the United Kingdom.”

Nobody knows why he wanted this, other than the fact that they were extremely sharp.

I’d just remove this sentence entirely.

The researchers did not follow protocol and assumed this confectionery item was safe to consume, similar to Product-H. However, the consumption of this resulted in the death of two researchers, involving poisoning by chlorine gas. The remaining researcher was reprimanded following the incident.

To me, this seems too unprofessional of a member of the Foundation. If a cupcake was created by a magic safe covered in a weird white powder, I don’t think a scientist would just take a big old bite out of that sucker.

He successfully consumed it, however the researchers were able to resuscitate him every time his heart stopped until medical personnel arrived. Testing involving D-1034 was permanently suspended.

I’d change this to, “Subject successfully consumed the arsenic, however researchers were able to stabilize subject until medical personnel arrived.”

Doctor Square had lost his keycard at the time, and had been desperate to find it. Containment procedures were revised following this, to prevent lower level personnel from obtaining higher level access.

I’d change this to, “Dr. Square had lost his keycard at the time, and was concerned about being repromanded for doing so. Following this, containment procedures were revised to prevent SCP-4865 from being used to find lost objects.”

The fat man" was later revealed to be an imaginary person, and D-8235 had previously unknown schizophrenia.

Probably change this to “undiagnosed schizophrenia” instead of “unknown schizophrenia”.

researchers discovered it flew a lot farther than what was thought possible.

I’d change it to “it flew much farther than expected.”

It is unknown what this means, though the phrase "take a shot" presumably means with a camera; not a gun.

The explanation of the phrase “Take a shot” seems unnecessary

Considering that D-1987 is responsible for the murder of █ women in the year 200█, it was presumed that this was one of his sickly ideas.

The formal tone really breaks here. Either overhaul this sentence or remove it entirely.

The arm was coated in what felt like a deep layer of rust, which eventually was grown over by skin during the days following the experiment.

Why wouldn’t they get a sample of this stuff instead of just saying it felt like rust? Also how was it grown over by skin?

Following an unexpected incident in which a penny was dropped in to the stainless steel item's slot and it subsequently appeared in SCP-4865, a test has been designated involving the object, giving the object designation SCP-4865-1

The penny is SCP-4865-1? How is it anomalous? What test involving it? This sentence is really confusing, as to why the penny is even anomalous, what test, what did the coin slot even do?

Again, I like this SCP. I just think it needs some tweaking and it would be great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pretty well done article my guy.

Only thing I really noticed wrong are some formatting issues. IE interview boxes usually have an extra line between dialogue lines and actions to avoid a cluttered look. On top of that, your description could do with a little trimming and reformatting of paragraphs. Otherwise, +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! I would never have noticed them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are two SCP-XXXXs in the table and other than that, this is great

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Although admittedly the article could use minor clean-ups to avoid confusion, it is otherwise original and well-structured and a very good first-time SCP.

+1

item.title: SCP-4865
item.content:encoded: Well hello there. This happens to be my first Scip. I have had it checked by people on the drafts and critiques forum as well as other people on several different platforms. They have given it the green light so I have decided to upload it.
